Main Content>4.2 Basic Trigonometric Functions>4.2.2 Trigonometric values of angles

mini_headings_covered: Relation between degree and radian measure

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: Relation between degree and radian measure
questions.question: The relationship \(\pi\) radians \(= 180^{\circ}\) is used for angle conversion. If an angle measures \(240^{\circ}\), what is its measure in radians?
questions.hint: Multiply degrees by \(\frac{\pi}{180}\) then simplify the fraction.
questions.explanation: The conversion formula \(\text{radians} = \frac{\pi}{180} \times \text{degrees}\) gives \(\frac{\pi}{180} \times 240 = \frac{240\pi}{180} = \frac{4\pi}{3}\). Option A omits the factor of 4, incorrectly using \(120^{\circ}\).

source_trace.structural_element: Relation between degree and radian measure

explanations_tiered.definition: Radian measure is the ratio of arc length to radius, where \(\pi\) radians equals 180 degrees.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Angles can be measured in degrees or radians. The fixed relationship \(\pi \text{ rad} = 180^{\circ}\) allows conversion between the two systems.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of \(\pi\) as 180°. To convert 240° to radians, ask: how many times does 180° fit into 240°? It fits 4/3 times, so answer is \(\frac{4\pi}{3}\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using the proportion \(\frac{\text{radians}}{\pi} = \frac{\text{degrees}}{180}\), substitute 240°: \(\frac{\text{rad}}{\pi} = \frac{240}{180} = \frac{4}{3}\). Multiply both sides by \(\pi\) to get \(\frac{4\pi}{3}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: From dimensional analysis: \(240^{\circ} \times \frac{\pi \text{ rad}}{180^{\circ}} = \frac{240\pi}{180}\text{ rad} = \frac{4\pi}{3}\text{ rad}\). Option A results from using \(120^{\circ}\) instead of 240°.
misconceptions: Some students incorrectly use \(\frac{180}{\pi}\) for degrees to radians, which gives degrees from radians.
Another error is forgetting to simplify the fraction, leaving \(\frac{240\pi}{180}\) as final answer.

glossary_entry.definition: An angle subtended at the center of a circle by an arc equal in length to the radius.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Angle measure where one radian is the angle subtended by an arc equal to the radius.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: multiplying by 180/π instead of π/180.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Convert between degree and radian measures

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is the radian measure of an angle that measures \(270^{\circ}\)?
questions.explanation: Using \(\text{rad} = \frac{\pi}{180} \times \text{deg}\), \(\frac{\pi}{180} \times 270 = \frac{270\pi}{180} = \frac{3\pi}{2}\). Option B comes from incorrectly using \(300^{\circ}\) instead of 270°.

explanations_tiered.definition: Radian measure is defined by \(\theta = \frac{l}{r}\) and the conversion \(\pi \text{ rad} = 180^{\circ}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Angles measured in degrees can be converted to radians using the fixed ratio \(\frac{\pi}{180}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a half-circle is π radians (180°). 270° is one and a half half-circles: \(\frac{3}{2} \times \pi = \frac{3\pi}{2}\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Set up proportion: \(\frac{\text{radians}}{\pi} = \frac{270}{180} = \frac{3}{2}\). Multiply both sides by π gives \(\frac{3\pi}{2}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Dimensional analysis: \(270^{\circ} \times \frac{\pi \text{ rad}}{180^{\circ}} = \frac{270\pi}{180} = \frac{27\pi}{18} = \frac{3\pi}{2}\). Option B corresponds to 300°.
reasoning_steps: Simplify fraction: divide numerator and denominator by 90 → 3π/2.
misconceptions: Some think 270° = 7π/4 because they confuse with 315°.
Others incorrectly multiply by 180/π instead of π/180.

glossary_entry.definition: An angle whose terminal side lies on an axis, e.g., 0°, 90°, 180°, 270°.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Angle with terminal side on x- or y-axis.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that quadrantal angles have exact radian multiples of π/2.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: Relation between degree and radian measure
questions.question: A central angle subtends an arc of length \(22\text{ cm}\) in a circle of radius \(14\text{ cm}\). What is the measure of this angle in radians? (Use \(\pi = \frac{22}{7}\))
questions.hint: Use the formula \(\theta = \frac{l}{r}\) where \(l\) is arc length, \(r\) is radius.
questions.explanation: The radian measure formula \(\theta = \frac{l}{r}\) gives \(\frac{22}{14} = \frac{11}{7}\) radians. Option A inverts the fraction, using \(\frac{r}{l}\) instead.

source_trace.structural_element: Relation between degree and radian measure

explanations_tiered.definition: One radian is the angle subtended by an arc equal in length to the radius, giving \(\theta = l/r\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The radian measure of a central angle equals the arc length divided by the radius, independent of circle size.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think: how many radius-lengths fit along the arc? Here arc is 22 cm, radius is 14 cm, so \(22/14 = 11/7\) radius-lengths → \(11/7\) radians.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The dimensionless radian measure \(\theta = l/r\) comes from circle geometry: circumference \(2\pi r\) gives \(2\pi\) rad. Here \(\theta = 22/14 = 11/7 \approx 1.571\) rad.
misconceptions: Some students incorrectly use \(\theta = r/l\), giving 14/22 = 7/11.
Others try to convert using π unnecessarily.

glossary_entry.definition: Angle measure given by arc length divided by radius.

flashcard.front: Arc length 22 cm, radius 14 cm. Find angle in radians.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Arc 22 cm, radius 14 cm. Angle in radians?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: inverting ratio to r/l.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Find radian measure from arc length and radius

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.sub_subsection: 4.2.1 The sine, cosine and tangent functions
questions.question: On the unit circle, a point \(P\) corresponds to an angle \(x\) and has coordinates \((\cos x, \sin x)\). If \(\cos x = -\frac{12}{13}\) and the terminal side lies in Quadrant II, what is the value of \(\sin x\)?
questions.hint: Use the identity \(\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1\) and consider the sign in Quadrant II.
questions.explanation: From \(\sin^2 x = 1 - \cos^2 x = 1 - \frac{144}{169} = \frac{25}{}\), so \(\sin x = \pm\frac{5}{13}\). In Quadrant II, sine is positive, so \(\sin x = \frac{5}{13}\). Option A gives the correct magnitude but wrong sign (Quadrant IV).

explanations_tiered.definition: The unit circle identity \(\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1\) holds for all real \(x\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For any point \((\cos x, \sin x)\) on the unit circle, the coordinates satisfy the Pythagorean identity because the radius is 1.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine the unit circle: x-coordinate is cos, y-coordinate is sin. If cos = -12/13, then by Pythagoras, sin² = 1 - (144/169) = 25/169, so sin = ±5/13. Quadrant II means y positive, so +5/13.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Use \(\sin^2 x = 1 - \cos^2 x = 1 - \frac{144}{169} = \frac{25}{169}\). Thus \(\sin x = \pm \frac{5}{13}\). Quadrant II: sine positive, cosine negative → \(\sin x = +\frac{5}{13}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Pythagorean identity derived from unit circle: \(x^2 + y^2 = 1\) with \(x=\cos x, y=\sin x\). Substituting \(\cos x = -12/13\) gives \(\sin^2 x = 1 - 144/169 = 25/169\). Sign determined by quadrant: II → sin positive.

formula_analysis.name: Pythagorean identity
formula_analysis.derivation: From unit circle \(x^2 + y^2 = 1\) with \(x = \cos x, y = \sin x\).

misconceptions: Forgetting to consider sign based on quadrant: students may give ±5/13 as final answer.
Confusing Quadrant II (sin positive) with Quadrant IV (sin negative).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Quadrant II: sin positive, cos negative. sin² = 1 - cos² → sin = +5/13.

flashcard.front: cos x = -12/13 in Quadrant II. Find sin x.
flashcard.back: 5/13, because sin² = 1 - 144/169 = 25/169, and sin positive in QII.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: cos x = -12/13 in QII. sin x = ?
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use Pythagorean identity to find sine given cosine and quadrant

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.sub_subsection: 4.2.1 The sine, cosine and tangent functions
questions.question: An angle \(\theta\) has \(\sin \theta = -\frac{3}{5}\) and \(\cos \theta = \frac{4}{5}\). In which quadrant does the terminal side of \(\theta\) lie?
questions.hint: Recall the sign patterns: sine negative in QIII and QIV; cosine positive in QI and QIV.
questions.explanation: Cosine positive and sine negative occurs only in Quadrant IV. Option C (Quadrant III) has both sine and cosine negative, not matching.

source_trace.content_anchor: Table of signs: QIV: sin -, cos +

explanations_tiered.definition: Quadrants determine the signs of sine (y-coordinate) and cosine (x-coordinate) on the unit circle.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In Quadrant IV, x is positive and y is negative, so cosine positive, sine negative.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of the unit circle: right side (x>0) is QI and QIV. Down side (y<0) is QIII and QIV. Overlap: QIV has x>0, y<0 → cos +, sin -.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Check sign patterns: QI (+,+), QII (-,+), QIII (-,-), QIV (+,-). Given sin negative, cos positive → QIV.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Unit circle coordinates: (cos θ, sin θ) = (4/5, -3/5). x positive, y negative → point in QIV.
reasoning_steps: Recall sign table: sin positive in QI, QII; sin negative in QIII, QIV.
cos positive in QI, QIV; cos negative in QII, QIII.
Intersection of sin negative and cos positive → QIV.
misconceptions: Some students think negative sine always means QIII, forgetting QIV.
Confusing cosine sign: in QII cos negative.

glossary_entry.definition: One of four regions of the coordinate plane divided by axes.

flashcard.front: sin θ = -3/5, cos θ = 4/5. Which quadrant?
flashcard.back: Quadrant IV, because cos positive, sin negative.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Sin negative, cos positive → which quadrant?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use mnemonic: All Students Take Calculus (ASTC) for positive signs.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine quadrant from signs of sine and cosine
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Cosine positive, sine negative → Quadrant IV.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If \(\sin 35^{\circ} \approx 0.574\), what is the value of \(\cos 55^{\circ}\)?
questions.explanation: The cofunction identity \(\sin \theta = \cos (90^{\circ} - \theta)\) applies: \(\sin 35^{\circ} = \cos (90^{\circ} - 35^{\circ}) = \cos 55^{\circ} \approx 0.574\). Option B might come from \(\sin 55^{\circ}\) confusion.

explanations_tiered.definition: Two angles are complementary if their sum is \(90^{\circ}\); for complementary angles, sine of one equals cosine of the other.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The cofunction identity connects sine and cosine of complementary angles: \(\sin \theta = \cos(90^{\circ} - \theta)\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: 35° and 55° add to 90°, so they are complements. For complements, sine of the smaller equals cosine of the larger.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From right triangle geometry: if one acute angle is 35°, the other is 55°. Opposite side of 35° equals adjacent side of 55°, so \(\sin 35^{\circ} = \cos 55^{\circ}\).
misconceptions: Some use sin θ = cos θ incorrectly.
Others try sin55° directly, which is cos35°, not cos55°.

glossary_entry.definition: Trigonometric function of a complement: sin θ = cos(90°-θ).

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use right triangle to visualize why sinθ = opposite/hypotenuse equals adjacent/hypotenuse of complement.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is the exact value of \(\sin 150^{\circ}\) using its reference angle?
questions.hint: Find the reference angle: \(150^{\circ}\) is in Quadrant II, reference = \(180^{\circ} - 150^{\circ} = 30^{\circ}\). Sine is positive in QII.
questions.explanation: The reference angle for \(150^{\circ}\) is \(30^{\circ}\). In Quadrant II, sine is positive, so \(\sin 150^{\circ} = +\sin 30^{\circ} = \frac{1}{2}\). Option B gives the correct magnitude but wrong sign (QIV).

explanations_tiered.definition: The reference angle is the acute angle between the terminal side and the x-axis, used to find trig values of non-acute angles.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For any angle, the absolute value of a trigonometric function equals that of its reference angle; sign depends on quadrant.
explanations_tiered.beginner: 150° is in QII, where sine is positive. Its distance to 180° is 30°, so reference = 30°. sin30° = 1/2, so sin150° = +1/2.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Reference angle: \(180^{\circ} - 150^{\circ} = 30^{\circ}\). Quadrant II: sine positive → \(\sin 150^{\circ} = +\sin 30^{\circ} = 1/2\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: On unit circle, 150° corresponds to point \((-\sqrt{3}/2, 1/2)\). y-coordinate = sine = 1/2. Reference angle 30° gives same sine magnitude.
misconceptions: Forgetting sign: some give -1/2 because they incorrectly apply cosine rule.
Using 30° but wrong sign from QII.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: QII: sin positive. Ref 30° → sin150° = sin30° = 1/2.

glossary_entry.definition: Acute angle between terminal side and x-axis.

flashcard.back: 1/2, because reference 30° and sine positive in QII.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw unit circle to show symmetry.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Evaluate trig functions using reference angles
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. 150° in QII, ref 30°, sin positive → 1/2.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If the reference angle for \(\theta\) is \(\frac{\pi}{6}\) and \(\tan \theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\), in which quadrant does \(\theta\) terminate?
questions.hint: tan is negative in Quadrants II and IV. The reference angle \(\pi/6\) matches the magnitude.
questions.explanation: The magnitude \(|\tan \theta| = \tan(\pi/6) = \sqrt{3}/3\). Since tan is negative, \(\theta\) must be in QII (where tan negative) or QIV (tan negative). Option C is too restrictive.

source_trace.content_anchor: tan negative in QII and QIV

explanations_tiered.definition: The reference angle gives the acute angle measure, and the sign of the trig function determines quadrant.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Given reference angle α, θ = α (QI), π-α (QII), π+α (QIII), 2π-α (QIV). Sign of tan picks QII (negative) or QIV (negative).
explanations_tiered.beginner: tan(π/6) = √3/3 positive. Our tan is negative, so θ cannot be in QI or QIII. That leaves QII or QIV, where tan is negative.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Reference angle π/6 means acute angle with tan = √3/3. tan θ = -√3/3 → negative. tan negative in QII and QIV → θ in QII or QIV.
explanations_tiered.advanced: tan θ = sinθ/cosθ. For ref π/6: sin=1/2, cos=√3/2. QII: sin+, cos- → tan -; QIV: sin-, cos+ → tan -. So both possible.
reasoning_steps: Given tan θ negative → θ in QII or QIV.
Thus θ in QII or QIV.
misconceptions: Thinking tan negative only in one quadrant (e.g., only QIV).
Confusing tan sign pattern with sine or cosine.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: tan negative → QII or QIV. Ref π/6 → magnitude matches.

glossary_entry.definition: Tangent is positive in QI and QIII, negative in QII and QIV.

flashcard.front: Ref angle π/6, tan θ = -√3/3. Quadrants?
flashcard.back: QII or QIV, because tan negative in those quadrants.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: tan θ negative, ref π/6. Quadrants?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ask students to find actual angles: 5π/6 and 11π/6.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine quadrant from sign of tangent and reference angle
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. tan negative in QII and QIV; reference π/6 gives magnitude.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: Relation between degree and radian measure
questions.question: The minute hand of a clock is \(1.2\text{ m}\) long. How far does the tip of the minute hand move in \(20\) minutes? (Use \(\pi = 3.14\))
questions.hint: In 60 minutes the hand sweeps \(2\pi\) radians. Find fraction of revolution for 20 minutes, then arc length \(l = r\theta\).
questions.explanation: 20 minutes is \(\frac{1}{3}\) of an hour, so angle \(\theta = \frac{1}{3} \times 2\pi = \frac{2\pi}{3}\) rad. Arc length \(l = r\theta = 1.2 \times \frac{2\pi}{3} = 0.8\pi \approx 2.512\text{ m}\). Option A uses only half the angle.

source_trace.structural_element: Relation between degree and radian measure
source_trace.content_anchor: minute hand completes 1 revolution in 60 minutes, l = rθ

explanations_tiered.definition: Arc length \(l\) equals radius \(r\) times angle in radians \(\theta\): \(l = r\theta\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The minute hand rotates at constant angular speed, sweeping \(2\pi\) rad in 60 minutes. The distance traveled by the tip is the arc length.
explanations_tiered.beginner: In 20 minutes, the hand covers 1/3 of a full circle. Full circle distance = circumference = 2πr. One-third of that = (2π×1.2)/3 = 0.8π ≈ 2.512 m.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Angular displacement in 20 min = \(\frac{20}{60} \times 2\pi = \frac{2\pi}{3}\) rad. Arc length = \(1.2 \times \frac{2\pi}{3} = 0.8\pi \approx 2.512\) m.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Angular speed ω = \(2\pi\) rad/60 min = \(\pi/30\) rad/min. In 20 min, θ = ωt = \(20\pi/30 = 2\pi/3\) rad. l = rθ = 1.2 × 2π/3 = 0.8π m.
misconceptions: Using θ = 2π directly without scaling by time fraction.
Using degree measure instead of radians in l = rθ.

glossary_entry.definition: Distance along a circle's circumference, l = rθ (θ in radians).

flashcard.front: Minute hand length 1.2 m. Distance in 20 min? (π=3.14)
flashcard.back: 2.512 m, because θ = 2π/3 rad, l = rθ = 0.8π = 2.512 m.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Minute hand 1.2 m, distance in 20 min?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: 2.512 m, l = rθ with θ = 2π/3 rad.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve real-life problems using arc length formula

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.sub_subsection: 4.2.1 The sine, cosine and tangent functions
questions.question: Which of the following is the correct definition of the secant function?
questions.hint: Recall that secant is the reciprocal of cosine.
questions.explanation: By definition, secant is the reciprocal of cosine: \(\sec \theta = \frac{1}{\cos \theta}\). Option A defines cosecant, option C defines cotangent.

explanations_tiered.definition: Secant is the reciprocal of cosine: \(\sec \theta = \frac{1}{\cos \theta}\), defined where \(\cos \theta \neq 0\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The three reciprocal functions are secant (1/cos), cosecant (1/sin), and cotangent (1/tan).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think: secant goes with cosine like a pair of shoes: sec = 1/cos.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From right triangle: cos = adjacent/hypotenuse, so sec = hypotenuse/adjacent = 1/cos.
misconceptions: Confusing secant with cosecant (1/sin).
Thinking secant is 1/tan.

glossary_entry.definition: Reciprocal of cosine: sec θ = 1/cos θ.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use memory trick: "sec" has 'c' for cosine.
